content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 14, 2015

Richard D. Land, P.E.
14159 Horseshoe Lane
Grass Valley, CA 95949

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-15-187

Dear Mr. Land:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental
employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that our advice is
based solely on the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application of other post-
government employment law. Also, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     Does the Act's "permanent ban" on certain post-governmental employment activities
prohibit you from working for AECOM on state construction inspection contracts 59A0937,
59A0938, and 59A0939 issued by the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans")?

CONCLUSION

     Provided that you did not previously participate in these contracts while working at
Caltrans, the Act's permanent ban does not prohibit you from working for AECOM on the
contracts.*

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² This conclusion is based on the facts presented, and in particular on your statement from your email dated
October 2, 2015, that "I had no involvement whatsoever in these contracts while working at Caltrans." We caution
that where a contract involves a project, any involvement in the project may constitute participation in the contract
for purposes of the Act's permanent ban and may also implicate the application of Section 1090. Accordingly, for
any contract involving a project in which you may have been involved, we encourage you to seek further advice
based on a detailed description of the full extent of your involvement.

File No. A-15-187
Page No. 2

FACTS

In April of 2014, you separated from state service after 36 years at Caltrans. Between
May 2011 and April 2014, you held the position of Chief Deputy Director at Caltrans and were
responsible for overseeing internal operations, including project delivery, maintenance and
operations, planning and modal programs, administration, finance, audits and investigations,
information services, and legal. Between January 2005 and May 2011, you held the position of
Deputy Director for Project Delivery/Chief Engineer and were responsible for overseeing the
delivery of the statewide capital program and managing the Divisions of Environmental
Analysis, Design, Right of Way and Land Surveys, Engineering Services, Construction, and
Project Management in the development of guidance, standards, training, and performance
measures.

Your primary responsibilities in these positions were to create general policies and
oversee the functions of Caltrans' programs. Subordinate employees multiple layers below your
positions were responsible for contract-specific decisions and work developing, advertising, and
administering state construction contracts, and you did not directly supervise the subordinate
employees involved in those tasks.

You are now employed as a Technical Practice Lead for Long Span Bridges by AECOM,
a private company that provides services related to infrastructure development. In that position,
you are considering potential work on state construction inspection contracts 59A0937,
59A0938, and 59A0939 issued by Caltrans. In your email dated October 2, 2015, you stated that
you "had no involvement whatsoever in these contracts while working at Caltrans." You were
involved in developing various other contracts for project construction work at times while at
Caltrans, but those contracts have long since been completed and closed out. In your email, you
also stated that you have not had any contact with Caltrans' employees in regard to the details of
specific work that AECOM does for Caltrans since separating from state service.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. In addition,
Section 87407 prohibits officials from making, participating in making, or using their position to
influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or have any
arrangement concerning employment. These provisions are commonly referred to as the
"revolving door" prohibitions. We do not address the one-year ban or Section 87407 because
your question implicates only the permanent ban.

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and
other specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former

File No. A-15-187
Page No. 3

state employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when the official has permanently left or takes a leave of
absence from any particular state office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).)³

For purposes of the permanent ban, a "state administrative official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official
responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely
clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." (Section 87400(b).) A "judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding" to which the ban applies, is "any proceeding, application, request for a ruling
or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or
other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative
agency . . .." (Section 87400(c).) Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in
a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally and substantially through
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information . . .. (Section 87400(d).)

Furthermore, "[t]he permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases
where the new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official
had participated. A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in
previous proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No.
I-03-119.) New contracts with the employer's former agency in which the former employee did
not participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. I-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or
approval. (Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.)

Application of the Permanent Ban

As Chief Deputy Director or Deputy Director for Project Delivery Chief Engineer at
Caltrans prior to separating from state service, you engaged in proceedings in other than a purely
clerical, secretarial, or ministerial capacity as part of your official responsibilities. (Section
87400(b).) Thus, for purposes of the permanent ban, you were a state administrative official, and
the permanent ban restricts your activities in the private sector.

Participation in Proceedings

However, the permanent ban only applies to proceedings in which you have "taken part
personally and substantially" while at Caltrans. (Section 87400(d).) You state that you had "no

³ For purposes of the permanent ban, "[t]he date on which an official permanently leaves office or
employment or takes a leave of absence is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the
duties of the office or employment, and the official stops performing those duties, even if the official continues to
receive compensation for accrued leave credits." (Regulation 18746.4(a)(1).)

File No. A-15-187
Page No. 4

involvement whatsoever in the contracts at issue while working at Caltrans." During your 36
years working at Caltrans, you were involved in developing various other contracts for project
construction work at times, but those contracts have long since been completed and closed out.
Therefore, based on the facts presented, you did not individually participate in the proceedings
(i.e., the contracts) at issue while at Caltrans for purposes of the permanent ban.

Participation in Proceedings through the Exercise of Supervisory Authority

Regulation 18741.1(a)(4) provides that a supervisor is deemed to have participated in any
proceeding that was (1) pending before the official's agency and (2) under his or her
"supervisory authority," and that a proceeding is under a supervisor's supervisory authority if
any of the following applies:

"(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within
the agency for directing the operation or function of the program where the
proceeding is initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply
to a supervisor who is only responsible for the general oversight of the
administrative actions or functions of a program in which the
responsibilities concerning the specific or final review of the proceeding are
expressly delegated to other persons in the agency.

"(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the
investigation, review, or other action in the proceeding including, but not
limited to, assigning the matter for which the required conduct is taken.

"(C) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the
proceeding regarding the subject of the proceeding."

As Chief Deputy Director of Caltrans, you were responsible for overseeing its internal
operations, and as Deputy Director for Project Delivery/Chief Engineer, you were responsible for
overseeing the delivery of the statewide capital program and for managing various Divisions in
the development of guidance, standards, training, and performance measures. Your primary
responsibilities in these positions were to create general policies and oversee the functions of
Caltrans' programs. Subordinate employees multiple layers below your positions were
responsible for contract-specific decisions and work developing, advertising, and administering
state construction contracts, and you did not directly supervise the subordinate employees
involved in those tasks.

In this case, you have stated that you had no involvement in the contracts whatsoever.
Moreover, the general oversight over those that have been expressly delegated the authority to
enter the contracts does not in itself constitute participation in a decision through supervisory
authority. Provided you had no contacts with the participants in the proceeding regarding the
subject of the proceeding, it does not appear that you participated in the proceedings (i.e., the
contracts) at issue through the exercise of your supervisory authority.

File No. A-15-187
Page No. 5

Accordingly, to the extent that you had no involvement in the contracts, we conclude that the Act's permanent ban does not prohibit you from working for AECOM on the contracts at issue because you did not previously participate in these proceedings prior to separating from state service.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

the Act's permanent ban does not prohibit you from working for AECOM on the contracts at 
issue because you did not previously participate in these proceedings prior to separating from 
state service.
